neighborhoods.name: Vieille Ville (Old Town)
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's medieval and Renaissance core, perched on a low hill above the left bank of the Rhône. Cobbled lanes, antiquarian galleries, ancient guild buildings, the cathedral and the seat of the Republic of Geneva all coexist in a compact half-square-kilometre. Quiet by Swiss standards even in peak season — the city's quieter, more cerebral counterpoint to the lake.

location.description: On the hill immediately south of the Rhône, bordered by Plainpalais to the south and the Rues Basses shopping district to the north.
borders: Place de Neuve and Parc des Bastions (west)

history.overview: Continuously inhabited since the Romans founded Genava on this hill above the Rhône. Became a fortified episcopal seat in the Middle Ages and the operational base of the Protestant Reformation under Calvin in the 16th century. The city walls were demolished in 1850, but the medieval street plan and most of the buildings survive intact.
history.significance: Birthplace of the Protestant Reformation, capital of independent Geneva for centuries, and the city's symbolic heart. Calvin preached at St. Pierre; the Declaration of the Rights of Man was drafted in the Hôtel de Ville's Salle de l'Alabama, which also hosted the founding of the Red Cross in 1864.
notable_events: Roman foundation of Genava (1st century BC)
Reformation declared in Geneva (1536)
Founding of the International Committee of the Red Cross at the Hôtel de Ville (1864)
Demolition of the city walls (1850)

transit: Largely pedestrianised — buses 36 and 3 skirt the edges
10-minute walk from Cornavin main station via Pont du Mont-Blanc
Closest tram stop: Place de Neuve (lines 12, 15, 18)
practical_info.safety: Among the safest neighbourhoods in Europe — well-patrolled, lit, and busy until late.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Mid-morning for the antique shops and cafés; golden hour for the cathedral tower view; December for the Escalade festival re-enactments.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent but steep — cobbled climbs from the Rhône to the cathedral plateau require comfortable shoes.

known_for: Longest wooden bench in the world (Promenade de la Treille)
best_for: Anyone wanting calm in a global city

attractions.name: St. Pierre Cathedral & Archaeological Site
attractions.description: Calvin's home cathedral, with a 157-step tower climb and an underground site exposing 2,000 years of layered history.
attractions.appeal: City's best panoramic view and the most important Reformation site in the world.

attractions.name: Place du Bourg-de-Four
attractions.description: Geneva's oldest square, used as a Roman trading post and now the social heart of the Old Town with cafés, fountains and antique shops.
attractions.appeal: Pure Old Town atmosphere without the museum entry fees.

attractions.name: Maison Tavel
attractions.description: Oldest house in Geneva, now the city history museum — home to the remarkable Magnin Relief of 1850s Geneva.
attractions.appeal: Free, intimate, and surprisingly moving social history.

dining.name: Café Papon
dining.known_for: Classic fondue, filets de perche and a sun-trap terrace below the cathedral wall

dining.name: Les Armures
dining.known_for: Geneva's most famous fondue institution, frequented by every visiting head of state for decades
dining.atmosphere: Heavy wood beams, candlelight, formal but warm.

activities.name: Reformation Walk
activities.description: Loop from the Reformation Wall to St. Pierre via Calvin's house and the Auditoire de Calvin.

activities.name: Cathedral Tower Climb
activities.description: 157 steps for the best panoramic view in Geneva — lake, Mont Blanc, the Jura and the Old Town rooftops.

stay_here_if: You want quiet, atmospheric streets within walking distance of everything
You're interested in Reformation history or Swiss culture
You prefer boutique hotels in historic buildings
You're happy to climb cobbled hills
avoid_if: You want a buzzy nightlife scene on your doorstep
You're travelling with mobility constraints (steep, uneven streets)
You're after a lakeside view from your window
insider_tips: The Promenade de la Treille is home to the longest wooden bench in the world (120 m)
Carved on the Place du Bourg-de-Four fountain are the dates of every major political event in Genevan history — worth a slow read
Most antique shops close on Mondays — plan around it

neighborhoods.name: Pâquis
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's most cosmopolitan, multi-ethnic and around-the-clock neighbourhood — three blocks deep along the right-bank lakefront. Migration over four generations has made it the city's culinary United Nations: Lebanese bakeries next to Sri Lankan canteens next to Sichuan noodle bars. By night it doubles as Geneva's red-light district, which keeps prices honest and the streets lively.

location.description: Right bank of the Rhône, immediately behind the Quai du Mont-Blanc and the main train station Cornavin.
landmarks: Mausoleum of the Duke of Brunswick

history.overview: Originally fishing hamlets and pastures outside the city walls, absorbed into Geneva in 1850. The 20th century turned it into the city's main hospitality and migrant district, anchored by the railway station and the grand lakefront hotels.
history.significance: Where most visitors first stay, and where Geneva's working-class and international identities have always met.
notable_events: Construction of Cornavin station (1858)
Inauguration of the Bains des Pâquis (1932)
Wave of post-war migration that built today's culinary scene (1960s–1990s)

transit: Cornavin train station is at the top of the neighbourhood
Mouettes (yellow boats) from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives every 10 minutes
practical_info.safety: Generally safe and busy. The Rue de Berne / Rue de Monthoux strip is the city's red-light area — well-lit and policed but not for everyone after midnight.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Late morning for fresh-baked manakish and coffee; sunset on the Bains des Pâquis pier; weekend brunch.
practical_info.walkability: Flat, dense, walkable — ten minutes from end to end.

known_for: Lakefront promenade and Bains des Pâquis
Lebanese, Sri Lankan and Eritrean restaurants
Affordable hotels close to the station
Best winter sauna in the city

attractions.name: Bains des Pâquis
attractions.description: Free public swim spot in summer, sauna and hammam in winter, with the city's best café crème all year round.
attractions.appeal: The most Genevan of Genevan rituals.

attractions.name: Brunswick Monument
attractions.description: Ornate Gothic Revival mausoleum on the lakefront, built to honour the Duke of Brunswick whose estate financed it.
attractions.appeal: Strangely Venetian on the Geneva shore — a great photo subject.

dining.name: Le Comptoir des Pâquis
dining.known_for: Honest mezze, natural wines and the city's friendliest service

dining.name: Le Relais de l'Entrecôte
dining.known_for: Single-dish menu of green-sauced entrecôte and frites — Paris exported intact
dining.atmosphere: Bistro classic, queues out the door at 7pm.

activities.name: Sunset on the Pier
activities.description: Coffee or apéro at the Bains kiosk as the Jet d'Eau lights up.

activities.name: Mouettes Crossing
activities.description: Take the yellow boat from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives and back — cheapest lake cruise in Europe.

stay_here_if: You're arriving by train and want a five-minute walk to your hotel
You want a diverse food scene at every price point
You want lake access without paying lakefront prices
You'll be out late
avoid_if: You're sensitive to street noise and tram bells
You're travelling with very young children who'll find the red-light strip confronting
You want a quiet, leafy retreat
insider_tips: The Bains des Pâquis sauna is mixed-gender — bring a swimsuit
The hidden Sri Lankan restaurants on Rue de Neuchâtel are cheaper and better than the tourist-facing places on Rue de Berne
Notre-Dame Basilica's evening choral concerts (Saturdays) are free

neighborhoods.name: Eaux-Vives
neighborhoods.character: Eaux-Vives is the lakeside left bank: leafy, residential, slightly more refined than Pâquis across the water. Quai Gustave-Ador and the English Garden anchor the lakefront, while inland the neighbourhood spreads into elegant 19th-century apartment blocks, embassies, and a tight grid of independent cafés and bistros around Rue de Montchoisy.

location.description: Left bank of the Rhône, immediately east of the Old Town and stretching along the lake.
landmarks: Jet d'Eau and Jetée des Eaux-Vives

history.overview: Vineyards and country houses until incorporated into Geneva in 1798, urbanised through the 19th century with the elegant Quai Gustave-Ador and the English Garden as showpiece projects. The new Léman Express train link (2019) put the neighbourhood on the regional rail map and triggered a wave of high-end residential development.
history.significance: The civic showcase of the lakefront, where every postcard photo of Geneva is actually taken from.
notable_events: Annexation into Geneva (1798)
Construction of the Quai Gustave-Ador (1851)
Installation of the Jet d'Eau (1891)
Opening of Léman Express at Eaux-Vives station (2019)

transit: Eaux-Vives station on Léman Express (3 min to Cornavin, 38 min to Annemasse)
practical_info.safety: Very safe at all hours, even in the parks.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Late afternoon for golden light on the Jet d'Eau; spring for the Jardin Anglais; summer evening picnics in Parc La Grange.
practical_info.walkability: Very walkable, flat along the lake. The southern slopes toward Champel are mildly hilly.

known_for: Quai Gustave-Ador and the Jet d'Eau
English Garden and the Floral Clock
Parc La Grange's rose garden and summer concerts
Embassy quarter and high-end residential blocks
New Eaux-Vives station and surrounding gastronomy
best_for: Visitors with kids (parks and the lake)
Anyone wanting that Mont Blanc photo

attractions.name: Jet d'Eau Jetty
attractions.description: Walk the pier to feel the spray of Geneva's signature water column.
attractions.appeal: Unrivalled photo op of Geneva's most famous landmark.

attractions.name: Jardin Anglais
attractions.description: Lakefront park with the famous Floral Clock and a beautiful jet-spray fountain.
attractions.appeal: Free, central, scenic — Geneva 101 in 30 minutes.

attractions.name: Parc La Grange
attractions.description: Geneva's largest rose garden (12,000 plants) and the venue for the free Musiques en été summer concerts.
attractions.appeal: Local park life at its best.

dining.name: Café du Soleil
dining.known_for: Frequently listed among Geneva's top fondue spots (1834 institution)

dining.name: Tsé Fung
dining.known_for: Michelin-starred dim sum and Peking duck inside the La Réserve hotel

activities.name: Lakefront Run
activities.description: A flat, scenic 8 km loop from the English Garden out to Genève-Plage and back.

activities.name: Floral Clock Selfie
activities.description: Geneva's most photographed flowerbed, replanted seasonally.

stay_here_if: You want a lakeside neighbourhood without the buzz of Pâquis
You're travelling with kids and want parks and the lake
You prefer residential charm over central commotion
You're using the Léman Express for day trips to France
avoid_if: You're on a tight budget — accommodation is pricier here
You need to be next to the Old Town museums
insider_tips: Musiques en été (free summer concerts in Parc La Grange) runs all of July and August
The Eaux-Vives waterfront has Geneva's only true sandy public beach (free) — Genève-Plage to the east is the pay version with pools and a high-dive
From Quai Gustave-Ador you get the best photo of the Old Town across the harbour

neighborhoods.name: Carouge
neighborhoods.character: An entirely separate municipality across the Arve from Geneva proper, founded by Italians in the 1780s and built on a generous Mediterranean grid. Pastel houses, internal courtyards, communal vines, and a Saturday market on Place du Marché — Carouge feels half a continent away from buttoned-up central Geneva. It's the city's craft, design and food district.

location.description: Two kilometres south-west of central Geneva across the Arve river.

history.overview: Planned and laid out in the 1780s by Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia as a Catholic, free-trade rival to Calvinist Geneva. The Sardinian engineers used Turin's grid as a template, with single-storey houses, communal vine pergolas in the courtyards, and a deliberate religious tolerance that drew artisans from across Catholic Europe.
history.significance: Switzerland's most successful piece of 18th-century urban planning and Geneva's enduring counterweight to Protestant rigidity. Joined Geneva canton in 1816 but kept its own commune.
notable_events: Foundation by Charter of Victor Amadeus III (1786)
Joining of the Canton of Geneva (1816)
Designation as a federally protected historic town (1995)

transit: Trams 12 and 18 from Place de Neuve (10 min)
practical_info.safety: Among the safest neighbourhoods in the canton.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Saturday morning for the market; Thursday evening when restaurants stay open later; December for Christmas in Carouge.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent — flat, grid-planned, mostly pedestrian streets.

known_for: Saturday market on Place du Marché

attractions.name: Place du Marché
attractions.description: The communal heart of Carouge, hosting markets Wednesdays and Saturdays, plus open-air concerts in summer.

attractions.name: Église Sainte-Croix
attractions.description: Neoclassical 1781 parish church reflecting Carouge's Catholic foundation, set on the central square.
attractions.appeal: Architecture and quiet, with regular free organ recitals.

dining.name: Café des Négociants
dining.known_for: Classic Lyonnaise bouchon-style cooking — onglet, andouillette, île flottante

dining.name: Olivier de Provence
dining.known_for: Truffle ravioli and lavender crème brûlée since 1969

activities.name: Saturday Market Browse
activities.description: Fruit, cheese, charcuterie and flowers — the city's best market morning.

activities.name: Artisan Studio Crawl
activities.description: Loop through the boutiques on Rue Saint-Joseph and Rue Saint-Victor for ceramics, goldsmiths and bookbinders.

stay_here_if: You want an authentic, lived-in feel away from tourist Geneva
You love food markets and bistros
You're staying multiple nights and want a residential base
You're travelling with a partner on a slow itinerary
avoid_if: You want to wake up and walk straight to the cathedral
You're only in town for a single night between flights
You need to be next to the lake
insider_tips: Many Carouge courtyards are open — push the heavy wooden doors to discover communal vine pergolas and gardens
Thursday is Carouge's quiet long-lunch day; many shops shut for two hours after 12:30
Christmas in Carouge (first weekend of December) is a charming local event — book a table well in advance

neighborhoods.name: Plainpalais & Quartier des Bains
neighborhoods.character: Geneva's student, market and contemporary-art neighbourhood, organised around the vast triangular Plaine de Plainpalais — once a parade ground, now a flea market site, festival venue, and the city's largest open public space. The university spills out at one end; the gallery-heavy Quartier des Bains anchors the other.

location.description: South-west of the Old Town, across the Boulevard du Théâtre.
landmarks: MAMCO (Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art)

history.overview: Originally a flood plain of the Arve used as a parade and execution ground until the 19th century, when the university and surrounding boulevards were built. The Quartier des Bains takes its name from the public baths once located there, and from the 1990s became Geneva's contemporary-art gallery quarter.
history.significance: Geneva's intellectual and creative engine — student life, contemporary art, public protest, and the city's biggest flea market all in the same square kilometre.
notable_events: University of Geneva founded by Calvin (1559); current Uni Mail campus opened 1992
Opening of MAMCO (1994)
Launch of the Nuit des Bains gallery night (2002)

transit: Tram 14 along Boulevard de la Cluse
Walkable from the Old Town in 10 minutes
practical_info.safety: Very safe, busy with students and joggers at most hours.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Wednesday and Saturday mornings for the food market; Sunday for the flea market; Thursday evenings during the Nuit des Bains gallery openings.
practical_info.walkability: Flat, broad pavements, excellent for walking and cycling.

known_for: Wednesday/Saturday food market and Sunday flea market
Cinema Spoutnik and the art-house circuit
best_for: Anyone who finds the Old Town too polished

attractions.name: MAMCO
attractions.description: Switzerland's largest museum of contemporary art, in a former factory at the heart of the Quartier des Bains.
attractions.appeal: Bold programming and free entry on the first Sunday of the month.

attractions.name: Patek Philippe Museum
attractions.description: Two storeys of horological history — see attractions entry for detail.

attractions.name: Plaine de Plainpalais Flea Market
attractions.description: Sunday flea market sprawling across the plain; among the largest in Switzerland.

dining.name: Brasserie des Halles de l'Île
dining.known_for: Set lunch menus, river terrace, gallery exhibitions inside

dining.name: Café du Cirque
dining.known_for: Filets de perche done well at a fair price
dining.atmosphere: Tiled and corner-pub, a local fixture.

activities.name: Nuit des Bains
activities.description: Three nights a year (spring/autumn) when 20+ galleries throw simultaneous openings.

activities.name: Plainpalais Sunday Flea Market
activities.description: Loop the entire plaine for second-hand books, vinyl, vintage clothing and oddities.

stay_here_if: You want a creative, less touristy base
You're interested in contemporary art
You like markets, cinema and student energy
You want lower hotel prices closer to the centre
avoid_if: You want lake views from your hotel window
You're staying only one night and want the Old Town on your doorstep
insider_tips: Wednesday is the second farmers' market day on Plainpalais — quieter and easier than Saturday
Nuit des Bains dates are published in early spring; if you can time a trip, do it
The University's Uni Mail building has a free public rooftop with one of the best southern views in town

neighborhoods.name: International Quarter (Nations)
neighborhoods.character: Leafy, calm, half-park, half-glass-and-concrete diplomatic complex around the Palais des Nations. The flags of every UN member state line Place des Nations, Daniel Berset's 12-metre Broken Chair stands as a monument to landmine victims, and within walking distance are the WHO, ICRC, WIPO, WTO and the Botanical Garden. Not a place to stay — but an essential half-day visit.

location.description: Two kilometres north of Cornavin along Rue de Lausanne; bordered by the lake to the east and the Geneva-Lausanne road to the west.
landmarks: International Red Cross & Red Crescent Museum

history.overview: Until the 1920s this was farmland and country estates. Selected as the seat of the League of Nations in 1920 and remained a global diplomatic capital after the UN moved into the building in 1946. Continuous expansion through the 1960s–2010s as new agencies arrived.
history.significance: Home of the United Nations Office at Geneva (UNOG), the second-largest UN duty station, hosting most disarmament and human-rights negotiations.
notable_events: Inauguration of the Palais des Nations (1936)
Reopening as the UN European Headquarters (1946)
Inauguration of the Broken Chair (1997)
Opening of the WHO Member-State Lounge (2025 expansion)

transit: Tram 15 from Cornavin to Place des Nations (10 min)
Bicycle path from the lakefront in 20 minutes
practical_info.safety: Calm and very safe. Some streets close during major UN sessions and require ID for access.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Weekday mornings to combine a UN tour with the ICRC Museum and Ariana Park.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent if linear — most sights cluster around Place des Nations.

attractions.name: Palais des Nations
attractions.description: Guided tours of the UN's European headquarters.
attractions.appeal: One of the most distinctive 20th-century institutional buildings in the world.

attractions.name: Broken Chair
attractions.description: Daniel Berset's 12-metre wooden chair with one shattered leg — a campaign against landmines.

dining.name: Restaurant Vieux-Bois
dining.known_for: The Geneva hotel school's public restaurant — excellent value classic French

dining.name: La Perle du Lac
dining.known_for: Lakeside terrace, regional produce, Mont Blanc views

activities.name: Diplomatic Walking Loop
activities.description: UN → ICRC Museum → Ariana Park → Botanical Garden → lakefront (3 hours).

activities.name: Free Botanical Garden
activities.description: Twenty-eight hectares of formal gardens and tropical greenhouses with a small wildlife collection.

stay_here_if: You're here for a UN meeting or conference
You want a calmer, greener base than central Geneva
You prefer corporate hotels with conference facilities
avoid_if: You want walking access to the Old Town and nightlife
You're on a short trip and don't want to spend evenings commuting
insider_tips: UN tour tickets require a passport and at least 48 hours advance booking
Ariana Park is free and home to peacocks who wander between sessions of the General Assembly
The ICRC Museum's audio guide is essential — without it you'll miss most of the depth

spring: Walk the lakefront from Pâquis to Eaux-Vives as the chestnuts come into leaf
Catch the Geneva International Motor Show or the Salon du Livre (April)
Browse the year's Saturday markets restarting in Carouge and Plainpalais
Hike the Salève ridge after the snow melts (April–May)
Watch the Bains des Pâquis pier reopen for swimming (May)
summer: Swim at Bains des Pâquis or Genève-Plage
Picnic in Parc La Grange during the free Musiques en été concerts (July–August)
Cycle the Lavaux vineyard route between Lausanne and Montreux
Watch Fête de Genève fireworks over the lake (early August)
Take the cable car up the Salève for sunset over the Alps
autumn: Drink new wine at the Caves Ouvertes weekends across the Geneva countryside (September)
Hike the Jura crest for sweeping autumn colour
Visit MAMCO and the Quartier des Bains galleries during Nuit des Bains (October)
Try seasonal game menus (chasse) in Old Town bistros
Watch the Course de l'Escalade running race weave through the Old Town (late November)
winter: Use the Bains des Pâquis sauna at sunrise
Day-trip to ski at Les Houches, Flaine or La Clusaz (1 hour by bus)
Eat fondue or raclette in the Old Town
Walk the Christmas markets in the Jardin Anglais and Carouge (December)
Watch the Escalade festival re-enactments and chocolate-pot smashing (December 11–12)
